subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: wind, temperature and precipitation can be used to document short-term weather changes that are observable. Georgia S1E1c: observe and measure current weather conditions with simple instruments and record temperature, precipitation and sky conditions.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: yearly weather changes (seasons) are observable patterns in daily weather changes. Georgia S1E1d: analyze data to identify seasonal patterns of change such as temperature and rainfall/snowfall.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: weather changes occur throughout the day and from day to day and can be described by wind, temperature and precipitation. Georgia S1E1a: represent data in tables/graphs to identify and describe different types of weather and their characteristics; overlapping content with a data-representation framing.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: the moon, sun and stars can be observed at different times of the day or night. Georgia SKE1a: classify objects according to those seen in the day sky, the night sky, and both.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: sun, moon and stars appear in different positions at different times of the day or night. Georgia SKE1b: model the changes that occur in the sky during the day, as day turns into night, during the night, and as night turns into day.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: the observable shape of the moon changes in size very slowly throughout the month. Georgia S2E2d: use personal observations to describe, illustrate, and predict how the appearance of the moon changes over time in a pattern; same content at a later grade with prediction added.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: objects can be sorted and described by the properties of the materials from which they are made. Georgia SKP1a: compare and sort objects made of different materials such as clay, cloth, plastic, wood, paper, and metal.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: objects and materials can be sorted and described by properties including color, size and texture. Georgia SKP1b: classify common objects according to their physical attributes (color, size, shape, weight, and texture).

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: some objects and materials can be made to vibrate to produce sound; sound is produced when objects vibrate. Georgia S1P1d: construct an explanation supported by evidence that vibrating materials can make sound and that sound can make materials vibrate.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things have specific characteristics and traits, and they grow and reproduce. Georgia SKL1a: use observations to recognize the differences between organisms and nonliving objects; both hinge on what characterizes living things, but Georgia frames it as a living/nonliving distinction.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things have specific characteristics and traits. Georgia SKL1b: model how a set of organisms and nonliving objects are sorted into groups based on their attributes; overlaps on using characteristics of living things, with Georgia adding nonliving objects.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things grow and reproduce. Georgia SKL2c: observe similarities and differences of offspring to their parents and to other members of the same species; touches the reproduction idea but from the narrower offspring-resemblance angle.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things have physical traits, some of which are observable structures. Georgia SKL2a: argue from evidence how animals can be grouped according to their features; both center on observable physical traits, though Georgia is grouping-focused and animal-only.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things are made up of a variety of structures and some traits are observable. Georgia SKL2b: argue from evidence how plants can be grouped according to their features; the plant counterpart of the observable-traits idea.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: some structures and behaviors of living things influence their survival. Georgia S3L1b: explain how external features and adaptations (camouflage, hibernation, migration, mimicry) allow animals to survive in their habitat; same traits-influence-survival idea at greater depth.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: time can be measured. Georgia SSKH3e: correctly use the time-measurement words day, week, month, year; both introduce units for measuring time.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: time can be measured. Georgia SSKH3c: correctly use today, tomorrow, yesterday; relative time vocabulary rather than units of measure, so the overlap is on marking time.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: heritage is reflected through diverse cultures and shown through arts, customs, traditions, family celebrations and language. Georgia SSKG1: describe the diversity of American culture by explaining the customs and celebrations of various families and communities.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: symbols and practices of the United States include the flag. Georgia SSKH2a: identify the national and state flags (United States and Georgia flags).

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: symbols and practices of the United States include the Pledge of Allegiance. Georgia SSKH2b: identify the Pledge of Allegiance as an American symbol/practice.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: symbols and practices of the United States include the National Anthem. Georgia SSKH2c: identify the Star Spangled Banner as the national anthem.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: terms of direction and distance, symbols and landmarks are used to talk about the relative location of familiar places. Georgia SSKG3: state the street address, city, state, and country where the student lives; both describe the location of familiar places, but Georgia targets absolute address rather than relative-location vocabulary.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: models and maps represent real places. Georgia SSKG2a: differentiate land and water features on simple maps and globes; applies the maps-represent-real-places idea to specific features.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: models and maps represent real places. Georgia SSKG2b: explain that maps and globes show a view from above; both establish how maps/globes represent the real world.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: models and maps represent real places. Georgia SSKG2c: explain that maps and globes show features in a smaller size; both address maps/globes as scaled representations of real places.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: humans depend on and impact the physical environment in order to supply food, clothing and shelter. Georgia SS3H1b: compare how American Indians in each region used their environment to obtain food, clothing, and shelter; the same depend-on-environment concept in a historical context.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: humans depend on and impact the physical environment. Georgia SS2G2b: describe how historic figures and the Muscogee (Creek) and Cherokee adapted to and were influenced by their environments; human-environment interaction, but tied to specific Georgia figures.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: individuals share responsibilities and take action toward common goals in homes, schools and communities. Georgia SSKCG2: describe positive character traits of good citizens such as honesty, courtesy, respect and self-control; both address being a contributing community member, but Georgia frames it as traits rather than shared responsibility.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: the purpose of rules and authority figures is to provide order, security and safety. Georgia SSKCG1a: explain how rules are made and why.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: rules provide order, security and safety in the home, school and community. Georgia SSKCG1b: explain why rules should be followed.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: purpose of rules and authority figures is order, security and safety. Georgia SS2CG1: define the concept of government and the need for rules and laws; same purpose-of-rules idea extended to government.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: individuals have many wants and make decisions to satisfy those wants. Georgia SSKE4: explain that people must make choices because they cannot have everything they want.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: decisions to satisfy wants impact others. Georgia SS2E1: because of scarcity, people must make choices that result in opportunity costs; extends the choices-have-consequences idea with the opportunity-cost concept.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: goods are objects that satisfy wants and services are actions that satisfy wants. Georgia SSKE3a: distinguish goods from services.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: goods are objects and services are actions that satisfy wants. Georgia SS1E1: identify goods that people make and services that people provide for each other; same goods/services distinction with a producer framing.
